items.subtopic: Oral Manifestations of Disease | DISEASES OF THE TEETH AND PERIODONTAL STRUCTURES (part 1)
items.title: Understanding the Timeline and Structure of Tooth Development
items.content: Tooth formation begins in utero during the sixth week of embryonic life, continuing until age 17. Deciduous teeth typically erupt by age 3 and are shed by age 13, while permanent teeth start to erupt at age 6 and fully develop by age 14, with third molars possibly emerging later.
keyPoints: Tooth formation starts in the sixth week of embryonic life.
Deciduous teeth usually erupt by age 3 and are shed by age 13.
Permanent teeth begin to erupt at age 6 and fully develop by age 14.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · Oral Manifestations of Disease | DISEASES OF THE TEETH AND PERIODONTAL STRUCTURES (part 1)

items.subtopic: Oral Manifestations of Disease | DISEASES OF THE TEETH AND PERIODONTAL STRUCTURES (part 1)
items.title: Pathophysiology and Management of Dental Caries
items.content: Dental caries begin as an asymptomatic infectious process, primarily caused by Streptococcus mutans. If untreated, the decay progresses through enamel to dentin, potentially leading to acute or irreversible pulpitis, necessitating root canal therapy or extraction.
keyPoints: Caries start with bacterial colonization on plaque.
Acute pulpitis causes sensitivity to percussion and temperature changes.
Irreversible pulpitis leads to severe pain and requires root canal treatment.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · Oral Manifestations of Disease | DISEASES OF THE TEETH AND PERIODONTAL STRUCTURES (part 1)

items.subtopic: Oral Manifestations of Disease | DISEASES OF THE TEETH AND PERIODONTAL STRUCTURES (part 1)
items.title: Complications of Periapical Infection
items.content: Periapical infection from pulpal disease can lead to abscess formation, granuloma, or cyst development. Severe cases may result in osteomyelitis, gumboil, or Ludwig's angina, with at-risk populations including the elderly and those with diabetes.
keyPoints: Periapical abscesses can cause pain on chewing.
Chronic infections may lead to granulomas or cysts.
Complications include osteomyelitis and Ludwig's angina.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · Oral Manifestations of Disease | DISEASES OF THE TEETH AND PERIODONTAL STRUCTURES (part 1)

items.subtopic: DISEASES OF THE TEETH AND PERIODONTAL STRUCTURES (part 2)
items.title: Recognizing Acute Necrotizing Ulcerative Gingivitis and AIDS-Related Periodontitis
items.content: Acute necrotizing ulcerative gingivitis presents with sudden gingival inflammation, ulceration, bleeding, interdental gingival necrosis, and fetid halitosis. AIDS-related periodontitis can resemble this condition or a more destructive form of adult chronic periodontitis.
keyPoints: Acute necrotizing ulcerative gingivitis involves sudden gingival inflammation, ulceration, bleeding, interdental gingival necrosis, and fetid halitosis.
AIDS-related periodontitis may resemble acute necrotizing ulcerative gingivitis or a more destructive form of adult chronic periodontitis.
Both conditions can lead to rapid destruction of periodontal tissues.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · DISEASES OF THE TEETH AND PERIODONTAL STRUCTURES (part 2)

items.subtopic: DISEASES OF THE TEETH AND PERIODONTAL STRUCTURES (part 2)
items.title: Prevention Strategies for Tooth Decay and Periodontal Disease
items.content: Promote preventive dental care, especially in high-risk populations such as those with hyposalivation, diabetes, alcoholism, tobacco use, Down syndrome, or limited access to dental services.
keyPoints: High-risk groups include individuals with hyposalivation/xerostomia, diabetics, alcoholics, tobacco users, and persons with Down syndrome.
Promote regular dental hygiene, professional cleaning, fluoride toothpaste, and electric toothbrushes for those with limited dexterity.
Address barriers such as cost, fear of dental care, and cultural/language differences to improve access.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · DISEASES OF THE TEETH AND PERIODONTAL STRUCTURES (part 2)

items.subtopic: DISEASES OF THE TEETH AND PERIODONTAL STRUCTURES (part 2)
items.title: Systemic Conditions Affecting Oral Health
items.content: Conditions like acromegaly, Paget's disease, and celiac disease can affect teeth and periodontium. Recognize oral manifestations such as malocclusion, enamel hypoplasia, and gingival overgrowth.
keyPoints: Acquired prognathism from acromegaly and deformities from Paget's disease can cause malocclusion.
Enamel hypoplasia may result from intrauterine infections, vitamin deficiencies, or tetracycline exposure during early childhood.
Celiac disease is linked to nonspecific enamel defects in children.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · DISEASES OF THE TEETH AND PERIODONTAL STRUCTURES (part 2)

items.title: Management of Oral Ulcers: Recognize Red Flags for Early Intervention
items.content: Oral ulcers are common, with recurrent aphthous and herpes simplex being the most frequent. However, chronic or painless ulcers lasting more than 2 weeks should raise suspicion for malignancy such as squamous cell carcinoma.
keyPoints: Most acute oral ulcers are painful and self-limited.
Recurrent aphthous and herpes simplex account for the majority of acute ulcers.
Chronic, painless ulcers >2 weeks suggest squamous cell carcinoma or dysplasia; early biopsy is critical.
High-risk sites include lower lip, floor of mouth, ventral/lateral tongue, soft palate-tonsillar pillar complex.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · DISEASES OF THE ORAL MUCOSA

items.title: Identifying Oral Manifestations in Hematologic and Nutritional Disorders
items.content: Oral manifestations can indicate underlying hematologic or nutritional disorders. Prolonged bleeding, petechiae, ecchymoses, and oral ulcers may suggest conditions like platelet dysfunction, leukemia, or vitamin deficiencies.
keyPoints: Bleeding should stop within 15 minutes after minor trauma; prolonged bleeding suggests clotting abnormalities.
Leukemia can cause gingival bleeding, ulcers, and enlargement.
Plummer-Vinson syndrome increases risk for oral squamous cell cancer.
Scurvy presents with swollen, bleeding gums and tooth loosening.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · DISEASES OF THE ORAL MUCOSA

items.title: Differentiating Nonodontogenic Oral Pain: Focus on Myofascial Pain and Temporomandibular Disorders
items.content: Nonodontogenic oral pain, such as myofascial pain or temporomandibular joint disorder, is often overlooked. These conditions can cause significant discomfort and are associated with stress-related bruxism.
keyPoints: Most nonodontogenic pain stems from masticatory muscles or the temporomandibular joint.
Myofascial pain is common in individuals exhibiting bruxism due to stress.
Temporomandibular disorders present with pain, limited movement, and joint sounds; osteoarthritis can be a cause.
Management includes anti-inflammatory medications, jaw rest, soft foods, and heat.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · DISEASES OF THE ORAL MUCOSA

items.title: Trigeminal Neuralgia: Recognizing and Differentiating from Dental Pain
items.content: Trigeminal neuralgia involves the fifth cranial nerve, potentially affecting the mandibular or maxillary branches. It can mimic dental pain but is distinguished by its neuropathic quality and triggers such as touching the lip or chewing.
keyPoints: Pain may be triggered by brushing teeth or chewing.
Distinguished from toothache by neuropathic pain quality.
Involves mandibular or maxillary branches of the fifth cranial nerve.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · NONDENTAL CAUSES OF ORAL PAIN

items.title: Burning Mouth Syndrome: Identifying Neuropathic Etiology and Management Options
items.content: Burning mouth syndrome presents without an identifiable cause, potentially linked to neuropathy. Treatment options include clonazepam, α-lipoic acid, and cognitive-behavioral therapy.
keyPoints: Occurs in the absence of identifiable causes like vitamin B12 deficiency or Candida infection.
May be neuropathic in etiology.
Clonazepam, α-lipoic acid, and cognitive-behavioral therapy can benefit some patients.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · NONDENTAL CAUSES OF ORAL PAIN

items.title: Understanding Xerostomia and Its Causes
items.content: Xerostomia, or dry mouth, is perceived when salivary flow is reduced by 50%. The most common cause is medication, particularly those with anticholinergic properties. Other causes include Sjögren's syndrome, chronic parotitis, salivary duct obstruction, diabetes mellitus, HIV/AIDS, and radiation therapy affecting the salivary glands.
keyPoints: Xerostomia occurs when salivary flow is reduced by 50%.
Common etiology: medications with anticholinergic properties.
Other causes include Sjögren's syndrome, chronic parotitis, and radiation therapy.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · DISEASES OF THE SALIVARY GLANDS (part 1)

items.title: Management of Acute Bacterial Parotitis
items.content: Acute bacterial parotitis typically affects postoperative, dehydrated, and debilitated patients. It is usually unilateral with Staphylococcus aureus as a common pathogen. Management includes antibiotic treatment when infection is suspected.
keyPoints: Typically unilateral in adults.
Common pathogens: Staphylococcus aureus (including MRSA).
Antibiotic treatment necessary for suspected bacterial infection.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · DISEASES OF THE SALIVARY GLANDS (part 1)

items.title: Survival Rates and Treatment of Malignant Salivary Gland Tumors
items.content: Malignant salivary gland tumors have varying survival rates based on the stage: 94% for local, 70% with regional spread, and 43% when distant. Surgical resection is primary treatment; radiation therapy may be used post-surgery or if surgery isn't feasible.
keyPoints: 5-year survival rates: 94% (local), 70% (regional), 43% (distant).
Primary treatment: surgical resection.
Radiation therapy for high-risk histologic types.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · DISEASES OF THE SALIVARY GLANDS (part 1)

items.title: Antibiotic Prophylaxis in Patients with Joint Replacements
items.content: Routine dental procedures do not generally necessitate antibiotic prophylaxis for patients who have undergone joint replacement surgery to prevent late prosthetic-joint infection. Exceptions may apply if the patient has experienced complications from joint replacement.
keyPoints: Antibiotic prophylaxis is generally not recommended before oral surgery or mucosal manipulation in patients with joint replacements.
Exceptions may be considered for those who have had joint replacement complications.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · DISEASES OF THE SALIVARY GLANDS (part 2)

items.title: Use of Vasoconstrictors in Patients with Cardiovascular Conditions
items.content: Vasoconstrictors can be safely used in patients with controlled hypertension and stable coronary heart disease, arrhythmia, or congestive heart failure, provided intravascular injection is avoided. The total epinephrine dose should not exceed 0.036 mg.
keyPoints: 2% lidocaine with 1:100,000 epinephrine can be used safely in controlled cardiovascular conditions.
Total epinephrine dose should be limited to 0.036 mg.
Precautions needed for patients on tricyclic antidepressants and nonselective beta blockers.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · DISEASES OF THE SALIVARY GLANDS (part 2)

items.subtopic: Oral Manifestations of Disease | DISEASES OF THE TEETH AND PERIODONTAL STRUCTURES (part 1)
items.question: Which bacteria is principally responsible for the colonization that leads to dental caries?
items.explanation: Dental caries usually begin as a destructive infectious process of the enamel, with Streptococcus mutans being principally responsible for bacterial colonization.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · Oral Manifestations of Disease | DISEASES OF THE TEETH AND PERIODONTAL STRUCTURES (part 1)

items.subtopic: Oral Manifestations of Disease | DISEASES OF THE TEETH AND PERIODONTAL STRUCTURES (part 1)
items.question: What is the primary material that forms the majority of a tooth's substance?
items.explanation: The majority of the tooth substance is formed by dentin, which surrounds a core of myxomatous pulp.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · Oral Manifestations of Disease | DISEASES OF THE TEETH AND PERIODONTAL STRUCTURES (part 1)

items.subtopic: Oral Manifestations of Disease | DISEASES OF THE TEETH AND PERIODONTAL STRUCTURES (part 1)
items.question: What condition can result from untreated dental caries reaching the tooth pulp?
items.explanation: Untreated dental caries that penetrate to the tooth pulp can lead to irreversible pulpitis and eventually pulp necrosis.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · Oral Manifestations of Disease | DISEASES OF THE TEETH AND PERIODONTAL STRUCTURES (part 1)

items.subtopic: Oral Manifestations of Disease | DISEASES OF THE TEETH AND PERIODONTAL STRUCTURES (part 1)
items.question: Which structure binds the tooth's cementum to the alveolar bone?
items.explanation: The periodontal ligament tenaciously binds the tooth's cementum to the alveolar bone.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · Oral Manifestations of Disease | DISEASES OF THE TEETH AND PERIODONTAL STRUCTURES (part 1)

items.subtopic: DISEASES OF THE TEETH AND PERIODONTAL STRUCTURES (part 2)
items.question: Which of the following conditions is associated with impaired neutrophil chemotaxis and appears in adolescents?
items.explanation: Localized juvenile periodontitis is particularly destructive and appears to be associated with impaired neutrophil chemotaxis.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · DISEASES OF THE TEETH AND PERIODONTAL STRUCTURES (part 2)

items.subtopic: DISEASES OF THE TEETH AND PERIODONTAL STRUCTURES (part 2)
items.question: Which of the following conditions can cause enamel hypoplasia in children?
options: Tetracycline given during the first 8 years
items.explanation: Tetracycline, when given in sufficiently high doses during the first 8 years of life, may produce enamel hypoplasia and discoloration.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · DISEASES OF THE TEETH AND PERIODONTAL STRUCTURES (part 2)

items.subtopic: DISEASES OF THE TEETH AND PERIODONTAL STRUCTURES (part 2)
items.question: Which condition is characterized by a red-purplish, granular gingivitis known as 'strawberry gums'?
items.explanation: A red-purplish, granular gingivitis (strawberry gums) is a pathognomonic sign of granulomatosis with polyangiitis.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · DISEASES OF THE TEETH AND PERIODONTAL STRUCTURES (part 2)

items.subtopic: DISEASES OF THE TEETH AND PERIODONTAL STRUCTURES (part 2)
items.question: What condition might cause rapid focal tooth loosening due to infection?
items.explanation: Rapid focal tooth loosening can be caused by Langerhans cell histiocytosis, among other rare conditions.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · DISEASES OF THE TEETH AND PERIODONTAL STRUCTURES (part 2)

items.question: Which condition is most commonly associated with chronic, relatively painless ulcers in the oral mucosa?
items.explanation: Chronic, relatively painless ulcers and mixed red/white patches should raise suspicion for squamous cell carcinoma.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · DISEASES OF THE ORAL MUCOSA

items.question: Which of the following is a major acquired disorder that produces painful oral ulcers failing to heal within 2 weeks?
items.explanation: Mucous membrane pemphigoid is a major acquired disorder that produces painful oral ulcers.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · DISEASES OF THE ORAL MUCOSA

items.question: What condition can cause gingival bleeding, ulcers, and enlargement in the context of hematologic malignancies?
items.explanation: All forms of leukemia, particularly acute myelomonocytic leukemia, can produce gingival bleeding, ulcers, and enlargement.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · DISEASES OF THE ORAL MUCOSA

items.question: Which condition is characterized by a red, burning tongue and may occur with pernicious anemia?
items.explanation: A red, burning tongue may occur with pernicious anemia and is also a symptom of B-group vitamin deficiencies.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · DISEASES OF THE ORAL MUCOSA

items.question: Which condition is characterized by pain that may be triggered by touching the lip or gingiva, brushing teeth, or chewing?
items.explanation: Trigeminal neuralgia can involve the mandibular or maxillary branch of the fifth cranial nerve and may be triggered by these actions.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · NONDENTAL CAUSES OF ORAL PAIN

items.question: Which condition is associated with pain behind the ear and on the side of the face before facial weakness develops?
items.explanation: Pain behind the ear and on the side of the face before facial weakness is an early symptom of Bell's palsy.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · NONDENTAL CAUSES OF ORAL PAIN

items.question: Which condition may cause aching in several upper molar or premolar teeth unrelieved by local anesthesia?
items.explanation: Aching in upper molar or premolar teeth unrelieved by local anesthesia may point to maxillary sinusitis.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · NONDENTAL CAUSES OF ORAL PAIN

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a major component of saliva?
items.explanation: Saliva contains water, mucin, and lysozyme but not hemoglobin.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · DISEASES OF THE SALIVARY GLANDS (part 1)

items.question: What is the most common etiology of reduced salivary flow leading to xerostomia?
items.explanation: The most common cause of reduced salivary flow is medication, especially those with anticholinergic properties.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · DISEASES OF THE SALIVARY GLANDS (part 1)

items.question: What type of therapy might be used to manage acute bacterial parotitis?
items.explanation: Acute bacterial parotitis is typically managed with antibiotic treatment.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · DISEASES OF THE SALIVARY GLANDS (part 1)

items.question: What percentage of salivary gland neoplasms does pleomorphic adenoma compose?
items.explanation: Pleomorphic adenoma composes two-thirds (approximately 66%) of all salivary neoplasms.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · DISEASES OF THE SALIVARY GLANDS (part 1)

items.question: Which condition is associated with a very low risk of osteonecrosis of the jaw when treated with oral bisphosphonates?
items.explanation: The risk of osteonecrosis with oral bisphosphonate therapy is very low, unlike high-dose aminobisphosphonates for conditions like multiple myeloma.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · DISEASES OF THE SALIVARY GLANDS (part 2)

items.question: What is the recommended waiting period before elective dental treatment after a myocardial infarction?
items.explanation: Elective dental treatments should be postponed for at least 1 month and preferably for 6 months after a myocardial infarction.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · DISEASES OF THE SALIVARY GLANDS (part 2)

items.question: For which patients is antibiotic prophylaxis generally not recommended before oral surgery?
options: Patients who have undergone joint replacement surgery
items.explanation: Antibiotic prophylaxis is generally not recommended before oral surgery for patients who have undergone joint replacement surgery.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · DISEASES OF THE SALIVARY GLANDS (part 2)

items.subtopic: Oral Manifestations of Disease | DISEASES OF THE TEETH AND PERIODONTAL STRUCTURES (part 1)
items.statement: The periodontium consists of gingivae, alveolar bone, cementum, and the periodontal ligament.
items.explanation: The periodontium includes these supporting structures that hold the tooth in place.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · Oral Manifestations of Disease | DISEASES OF THE TEETH AND PERIODONTAL STRUCTURES (part 1)

items.subtopic: Oral Manifestations of Disease | DISEASES OF THE TEETH AND PERIODONTAL STRUCTURES (part 1)
items.statement: Pulp necrosis results in a loss of sensitivity to cold.
items.explanation: Once pulp necrosis is complete, pain may be constant or intermittent, but cold sensitivity is lost.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · Oral Manifestations of Disease | DISEASES OF THE TEETH AND PERIODONTAL STRUCTURES (part 1)

items.subtopic: DISEASES OF THE TEETH AND PERIODONTAL STRUCTURES (part 2)
items.statement: Tetracycline causes permanent tooth staining in children.
items.explanation: Doxycycline does not cause permanent tooth staining in children despite warnings for all tetracycline-class antibiotics.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · DISEASES OF THE TEETH AND PERIODONTAL STRUCTURES (part 2)

items.subtopic: DISEASES OF THE TEETH AND PERIODONTAL STRUCTURES (part 2)
items.statement: Patients with uncontrolled diabetes mellitus are at risk of severe periodontal disease.
items.explanation: Severe periodontal disease occurs in patients with uncontrolled diabetes mellitus.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · DISEASES OF THE TEETH AND PERIODONTAL STRUCTURES (part 2)

items.statement: Recurrent aphthous ulcers are often the presenting symptom in Behcet's syndrome.
items.explanation: Aphthous lesions can be a presenting symptom of Behcet's syndrome.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · DISEASES OF THE ORAL MUCOSA

items.statement: Herpes simplex ulcers are typically painless and persistent.
items.explanation: Most acute herpes simplex ulcers are painful and self-limited, not typically painless or persistent.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · DISEASES OF THE ORAL MUCOSA

items.statement: Glossopharyngeal neuralgia is triggered by pressure on the tragus of the ear.
items.explanation: Pressure on the tragus can trigger pain in glossopharyngeal neuralgia.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · NONDENTAL CAUSES OF ORAL PAIN

items.statement: Coronary ischemia always produces pain in both the face and jaw.
items.explanation: Coronary ischemia may produce pain exclusively in the face and jaw, not necessarily both.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · NONDENTAL CAUSES OF ORAL PAIN

items.statement: The sensation of a dry mouth is perceived when salivary flow is reduced by more than 75%.
items.explanation: Xerostomia, or the sensation of a dry mouth, is perceived when salivary flow is reduced by 50%, not more than 75%.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · DISEASES OF THE SALIVARY GLANDS (part 1)

items.statement: Pilocarpine and cevimeline can be used to increase salivary secretion when sufficient exocrine tissue remains.
items.explanation: Both pilocarpine and cevimeline are medications that can stimulate salivary secretion if there is enough remaining exocrine tissue.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · DISEASES OF THE SALIVARY GLANDS (part 1)

items.statement: Evidence supports that late prosthetic-joint infection follows routine dental procedures.
items.explanation: There is a lack of evidence that late prosthetic-joint infection follows routine dental procedures.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · DISEASES OF THE SALIVARY GLANDS (part 2)

items.statement: Vasoconstrictors should be avoided in patients with controlled hypertension and stable coronary heart disease.
items.explanation: If intravascular injection is avoided, vasoconstrictors can be used safely in these patients.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · DISEASES OF THE SALIVARY GLANDS (part 2)

items.subtopic: Oral Manifestations of Disease | DISEASES OF THE TEETH AND PERIODONTAL STRUCTURES (part 1)
items.assertion: Chronic periodontitis can lead to tooth loosening.
items.reason: Destruction of the periodontium, including supporting bone, causes teeth to loosen.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · Oral Manifestations of Disease | DISEASES OF THE TEETH AND PERIODONTAL STRUCTURES (part 1)

items.subtopic: Oral Manifestations of Disease | DISEASES OF THE TEETH AND PERIODONTAL STRUCTURES (part 1)
items.assertion: Dental caries primarily affect the dentin layer.
items.reason: Caries begin as a destructive process in the enamel before affecting the underlying dentin.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain that dental caries initially affect the enamel.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · Oral Manifestations of Disease | DISEASES OF THE TEETH AND PERIODONTAL STRUCTURES (part 1)

items.subtopic: DISEASES OF THE TEETH AND PERIODONTAL STRUCTURES (part 2)
items.assertion: Acute necrotizing ulcerative gingivitis presents with sudden gingival inflammation, ulceration, and fetid halitosis.
items.reason: This condition is a virulent example of rapidly progressive periodontal disease.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why acute necrotizing ulcerative gingivitis presents with these symptoms.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · DISEASES OF THE TEETH AND PERIODONTAL STRUCTURES (part 2)

items.subtopic: DISEASES OF THE TEETH AND PERIODONTAL STRUCTURES (part 2)
items.assertion: Linear gingival erythema is a condition seen in patients with advanced HIV infection.
items.reason: It represents immune deficiency and decreased neutrophil activity.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why linear gingival erythema occurs in these patients.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · DISEASES OF THE TEETH AND PERIODONTAL STRUCTURES (part 2)

items.assertion: Chronic oral ulceration should prompt consideration of squamous cell carcinoma.
items.reason: Early-stage malignancy is more treatable than late-stage disease.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why early diagnosis is important.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · DISEASES OF THE ORAL MUCOSA

items.assertion: Mucous membrane pemphigoid can be distinguished from lichen planus by clinical features alone.
items.reason: Biopsy or immunohistochemical examination is necessary to diagnose these entities accurately.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because biopsy or immunohistochemical examination is needed for accurate diagnosis, not just clinical features.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · DISEASES OF THE ORAL MUCOSA

items.assertion: Patients on warfarin anticoagulation rarely experience uncontrolled bleeding during dental procedures.
items.reason: Warfarin anticoagulation is maintained within the therapeutic range recommended for preventing venous thrombosis, atrial fibrillation, or mechanical heart valve issues.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why uncontrolled bleeding is rare.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · DISEASES OF THE SALIVARY GLANDS (part 1)

items.assertion: Prophylactic antibiotics are recommended for all patients undergoing dental procedures.
items.reason: Guidelines recommend prophylactic antibiotics only for high-risk patients undergoing specific dental and oral procedures.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because prophylactic antibiotics are not recommended for all patients, but the reason correctly states current guidelines.
items.reference: Oral Manifestations of Disease · DISEASES OF THE SALIVARY GLANDS (part 1)
